verdict_reasoning: C3 (complement component 3) is a secreted plasma protein produced mainly by the liver and locally by macrophages/other cells. Its canonical biology is entirely extracellular/soluble: it circulates as a zymogen, is cleaved into C3a and C3b, and C3b can covalently deposit onto surfaces via thioester-mediated attachment to hydroxyl/amine groups on pathogens, apoptotic cells, or ECM — but this is matrix/pathogen deposition, not stable anchoring to a host cell's own TM partner. No TM domain, no GPI anchor, no outer-leaflet lipidation. Contextual checks: (1) no documented cancer/cell-death ecto-pool on outer leaflet of host cells; (2) no germline/developmental surface display with own anchor; (3) not a lysosomal TM protein; (4) no documented PM minority pool or secretory cycling to PM; (5) thioester covalent deposition onto extracellular acceptors (pathogen/ECM surfaces) does not constitute stable TM-partner anchoring on a host cell surface — this is secreted_only territory. C3b opsonization of host cells is transient and washes off; the host PM TM partner story belongs to complement receptors (CR1, CR3), not C3 itself.
